sec-comments.title: SEC Comment Letters — The Full Breakdown
sec-comments.subtitle: Why the government's letters to companies are basically cheat codes

sections.heading: What Even IS EDGAR?
sections.body:
EDGAR stands for **Electronic Data Gathering, Analysis, and Retrieval**. It's the SEC's free public database — basically a massive digital filing cabinet where every public company (Apple, Tesla, GameStop, all of them) is legally required to dump their financial reports.

The SEC is the **Securities and Exchange Commission** — the federal agency that watches over Wall Street like a hall monitor. They were created after the 1929 stock market crash to make sure companies can't scam investors.

EDGAR has existed since 1996. Before that, you had to physically go to a SEC office in Washington DC and photocopy documents. Nightmare.

**Fun fact:** The name "EDGAR" was originally just a project acronym, but it stuck. There's no actual person named Edgar. Sorry.

sections.heading: So What Are Comment Letters?
sections.body:
Here's the flow:

1. A company files their quarterly or annual report on EDGAR
2. SEC staff (called "reviewers") read through it
3. If something looks sketchy, confusing, or incomplete, they send a **comment letter** — basically a formal "hey, explain yourself" letter
4. The company has to respond with clarification
5. Back-and-forth continues until the SEC is satisfied
6. **ALL of this is public record on EDGAR**

Think of it like this: You submit your homework. Your teacher writes "explain question 3" in red pen. You have to write a response. Except this is a billion-dollar company and the teacher is a federal regulator, and the homework is whether they're cooking their books.

sections.heading: Why Should I Care?
sections.body:
Because comment letters are **early warning signals**.

Academic studies have shown that companies receiving certain types of SEC comments are significantly more likely to:
- Restate their earnings later (admitting their numbers were wrong)
- Get sued by investors
- See their stock price drop
- Go bankrupt (in extreme cases, like going-concern comments)

**Exhibit A: The Going Concern Bomb**

If the SEC asks "does this company have enough cash to survive the next 12 months?" — that's a GOING CONCERN warning. It's the nuclear option. The SEC is basically saying "we're not sure this company is going to make it."

Companies that receive going concern warnings have an **80% chance of bankruptcy within 2 years**. That's not a typo. Eighty percent.

**Exhibit B: Revenue Recognition**

If the SEC questions HOW a company counts its revenue, pay attention. This is how Enron fooled everyone for years — they were counting revenue before they actually earned it. Like marking your test as "A+" before taking it.

**Exhibit C: Material Weakness**

When the SEC says a company has "material weakness in internal controls," it means their financial reporting systems are broken. You literally can't trust the numbers they're showing you.

sections.heading: How Hedge Funds Use This
sections.body:
Big hedge funds pay **$100K-$500K/year** for premium tools that basically just read EDGAR faster and add analytics. Services like RavenPack, Sentieo, and others charge insane premiums for what amounts to "we read the SEC comments before you do."

Our system does the same thing for free. The data is public. The SEC publishes it for free. We just automated the boring reading part.

**The edge isn't the data — it's the speed of reading it.** If you see a comment letter before the market fully digests it, you have a window where the stock hasn't moved yet. That's the opportunity.

sections.heading: How to Trade These Signals
sections.body:
**Difficulty: Easy to Medium**
**Capital needed: $100-$10K**
**Timeframe: 2-8 weeks (high severity) or 1-3 months (medium)**

Step-by-step:
1. Check the signal card for materiality score (7+ = serious)
2. Read the specific questions the SEC asked
3. Look at the company's response (if available)
4. Decide: Is this a real problem or just routine SEC review?
5. If real problem → consider shorting or staying away
6. If routine → the stock might actually be a buy (overreaction)

**Golden rule:** Not every comment letter means doom. The SEC sends hundreds per year. Most are routine. The art is knowing which ones matter. That's what our scoring system does for you.

patent-cliffs.title: Patent Cliffs — When Drug Monopolies Die
patent-cliffs.subtitle: Why knowing expiration dates can make you a fortune

sections.heading: The Basic Concept
sections.body:
When a pharma company invents a new drug, they get a **20-year patent**. That means NOBODY else can make that drug for 20 years. Total monopoly. They can charge whatever they want.

When the patent expires? **Game over for the brand name.** Any company can now make a "generic" version — same drug, same formula, but costs 80-90% less.

This is called a **patent cliff** because brand-name sales FALL OFF A CLIFF when generics enter.

**Exhibit A: Lipitor (Pfizer)**
- Peak sales: **$12.4 BILLION per year** (2006)
- Patent expired: November 2011
- Sales within 1 year: **$2.3 billion**
- That's an **82% drop** in revenue from one drug

**Exhibit B: Humira (AbbVie)**
- Peak sales: **$21 billion per year**
- Patent cliffs started: 2023
- Multiple biosimilar competitors entered
- Revenue expected to drop 30-40% by 2025

**Exhibit C: Keytruda (Merck)**
- World's best-selling drug: **$25 billion/year**
- Patent expires: 2028
- Merck is desperately trying to extend or find replacements

sections.heading: Why Does This Matter to You?
sections.body:
Because the market doesn't always price in patent cliffs immediately. Sometimes there's a 6-18 month window where:
- The cliff is approaching (publicly known)
- Wall Street analysts are still modeling peak sales
- The stock hasn't dropped yet

**If you know a patent cliff is coming, and the market hasn't priced it in, you can short the stock and profit from the crash.**

But it's not that simple:
- Companies try to extend patents (evergreening)
- New indications can extend revenue
- Buyouts and acquisitions can change the math
- Sometimes the cliff is already priced in

**That's why we track this.** We show you what's coming, when, and how severe.

sections.heading: The Patent Cliff Timeline
sections.body:
```
┌────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│              THE PATENT CLIFF LIFECYCLE                     │
├────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                             │
│  YEAR 0: Drug Approved                                      │
│  ├── Patent protection begins                               │
│  ├── Monopoly pricing: $10,000/month treatment              │
│  └── Stock rises on blockbuster potential                   │
│                                                             │
│  YEAR 10: Mid-life                                          │
│  ├── Sales peak: $10-20 billion/year                        │
│  ├── Analysts love it                                       │
│  └── Patent cliff is "on the radar" but distant             │
│                                                             │
│  YEAR 15-18: Cliff Approaching                              │
│  ├── Generics manufacturers file for approval               │
│  ├── Lawsuits begin (patent challenges)                     │
│  ├── Smart money starts shorting                            │
│  └── Stock starts declining                                 │
│                                                             │
│  YEAR 20: PATENT EXPLODES                                   │
│  ├── Generics flood the market                              │
│  ├── Brand price drops 50-90%                               │
│  ├── Sales crash 80%                                        │
│  └── Stock drops 20-80%                                     │
│                                                             │
│  YEAR 20+: New Normal                                       │
│  ├── Brand survives on loyalty + new formulations           │
│  ├── Revenue stabilizes at 20% of peak                      │
│  └── Company must find new drugs or die                     │
│                                                             │
└────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

sections.heading: How to Trade Patent Cliffs
sections.body:
**Difficulty: Medium to Hard**
**Capital needed: $1K-$10K+**
**Timeframe: 6-18 months**

The playbook:
1. Identify drugs expiring in the next 1-3 years
2. Check the brand-name company's revenue dependence on that drug
3. Look for "cliff hedging" — are they already diversifying?
4. If over-dependent and cliff is close → short opportunity
5. If already priced in → look for generic companies to go LONG

**Generic companies WIN from patent cliffs.** Companies like Teva, Sandoz, and Mylan make their living off these expirations. When a major cliff approaches, generics stock up.

**Pro tip:** The FDA maintains a "Orange Book" — the official list of drug patents and exclusivity. It's public. We cross-reference it with our data.

insider-trading.title: Insider Trading — When the Boss Bets on Himself
insider-trading.subtitle: Why CEO stock purchases are the most underrated signal in investing

sections.heading: What Is Insider Trading (The Legal Kind)?
sections.body:
"Insider trading" sounds illegal, but the legal version is totally normal and actually one of the best signals in investing.

**Legal insider trading:** When a CEO, director, or officer of a company buys or sells their OWN company's stock. They're required by law to report it within 2 business days on a form called **Form 4**. It's public record on EDGAR.

**Illegal insider trading:** When someone trades based on material non-public information (like knowing a merger is happening before it's announced). That's the kind that sends people to prison.

We only track the legal kind. The form 4 filings. The ones where the boss is putting their own money on the line.

sections.heading: Why Do Insiders Trade?
sections.body:
There are two types of insider activity:

**Buying (BULLISH signal):**
When a CEO buys their own stock, they're saying "I think this company is going up." They know the business better than any Wall Street analyst. They see the quarterly numbers before they're public. They know about new products, cost savings, upcoming deals.

Academic research shows insider buying beats the market by **6-8% per year** on average.

**Selling (less informative):**
Insiders sell for all kinds of reasons — diversification, college tuition, buying a house, pre-planned sales (10b5-1 forms). Selling doesn't necessarily mean bad news.

**Cluster buying (STRONGEST signal):**
When 3+ insiders buy at the same time? That's a cluster buy. It's one of the strongest signals in finance. Multiple insiders all voting with their wallets simultaneously = they all know something good is coming.

sections.heading: The Research
sections.body:
Studies going back decades show:

- **Seyhun (1986):** Insiders earn ~5% abnormal returns on their purchases
- **Jeng et al. (2003):** Insider purchases outperform by 6.2% annually
- **Cohen et al. (2012):** "Informative" buys (not routine) show even stronger returns

The pattern: Insiders who buy aggressively (large amounts relative to their holdings) show the strongest future stock performance.

**Why doesn't everyone just copy insiders?**
1. Most people don't know how to read Form 4 filings
2. The data is buried in EDGAR's messy index files
3. There's a lot of noise (routine sales, tax-related selling)
4. The edge is small — 6-8% annually — so it's not a get-rich-quick scheme

That's exactly why we filter and score it for you.

sections.heading: The System
sections.body:
```
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│              INSIDER TRADING DETECTION                   │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                          │
│  CEO/Director buys $500K of company stock                │
│         │                                                │
│         ▼                                                │
│  Form 4 filed within 48 hours (PUBLIC on EDGAR)          │
│         │                                                │
│         ▼                                                │
│  Our bot scans EDGAR index every 3 hours                 │
│         │                                                │
│         ▼                                                │
│  Is this a CLUSTER? (3+ insiders same period)            │
│         │                                                │
│    ┌────┴────┐                                           │
│    │ YES     │ NO                                        │
│    ▼         ▼                                           │
│  STRONG    NORMAL                                        │
│  SIGNAL    SIGNAL                                        │
│  (Score 8+) (Score 4-7)                                  │
│    │         │                                           │
│    ▼         ▼                                           │
│  ┌──────────────────────────────────────┐               │
│  │  YOUR DASHBOARD:                     │               │
│  │  • Company name + ticker             │               │
│  │  • Who bought + their title          │               │
│  │  • How much they spent               │               │
│  │  • Price per share                   │               │
│  │  • Cluster? (yes/no)                 │               │
│  │  • Difficulty + Capital needed       │               │
│  └──────────────────────────────────────┘               │
│                                                          │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

sections.heading: How to Trade Insider Signals
sections.body:
**Difficulty: Easy**
**Capital needed: $100-$1K**
**Timeframe: 1-6 months**

The playbook:
1. Look for cluster buys (3+ insiders) — strongest signal
2. Check the size of the purchase relative to their holdings
3. CEO purchases > Director purchases (CEOs know more)
4. Buy when insiders buy, hold for 3-6 months
5. Set stop losses — insiders can be wrong too

**Red flags to watch for:**
- Insider buying but company is simultaneously issuing bad news (could be a distraction)
- Very small purchases (token amounts just for optics)
- Selling right after buying (look at the full picture)

**The insider trading edge isn't about one trade. It's about a strategy of consistently following smart money over time.**

going-concern.title: Going Concern Warnings — The Nuclear Red Flag
going-concern.subtitle: When the SEC says a company might not survive, pay attention

sections.heading: What Is a Going Concern Warning?
sections.body:
A going concern warning is the most serious thing the SEC can say about a company.

In accounting, "going concern" means a company is assumed to be able to keep operating for the foreseeable future. It's a basic assumption behind all financial statements.

When the SEC reviews a company and thinks "this company might not survive the next 12 months," they require the company to disclose this in their filing. It's literally the SEC saying: **"We're not sure this business is going to exist a year from now."**

This is not a prediction. It's a regulatory determination based on the company's financial position — cash flow, debt levels, ability to fund operations.

sections.heading: Why Is This So Serious?
sections.body:
The numbers don't lie:

- **80% of companies that receive going concern warnings file for bankruptcy within 2 years**
- Stock prices typically drop **30-60%** within 30 days of disclosure
- Creditors may call in loans
- Customers and suppliers may flee
- Key employees often start leaving
- The company's ability to raise new capital dries up

**It's a death spiral.** And the SEC is basically putting up a giant neon sign that says "this company might be dying."

**Famous examples:**
- **Enron (2001):** Going concern warnings appeared months before the full fraud was exposed. Stock went from $90 to $0.61
- **Lehman Brothers (2008):** Substitute "going concern" with "liquidity crisis" — same result
- **Wirecard (2020):** Going concern issues emerged before the €1.9 billion hole was discovered. Stock dropped 99% in 3 days

sections.heading: How to Spot Them
sections.body:
Going concern warnings appear in:
- 10-K annual reports (Item 7 — Management's Discussion)
- 10-Q quarterly reports
- Sometimes in 8-K material event filings

Look for phrases like:
- "Substantial doubt about the entity's ability to continue as a going concern"
- "Going concern uncertainty"
- "May not have sufficient liquidity to fund operations"
- "Recurring operating losses and negative cash flows"

Our system flags these automatically and scores them HIGH materiality (8-10).

sections.heading: What to Do When You See One
sections.body:
**Difficulty: Easy (for selling) / Hard (for shorting)**
**Capital needed: $0 if you already hold the stock**
**Timeframe: Days to weeks**

If you own the stock:
1. Read the filing — understand WHY they're warning
2. Is it temporary (one-time charge) or structural (burning cash)?
3. If structural → SELL. Don't wait. Don't hope. Sell.
4. If temporary — monitor closely, set tight stop losses

If you don't own it:
- Consider shorting (but be careful — short squeezes happen)
- Or just avoid it entirely

**The golden rule of going concerns: The 80% bankruptcy rate is not a suggestion. It's a warning. Respect it.**

potential-earnings.title: Potential Earnings — What Can You Actually Make?
potential-earnings.subtitle: Realistic math on signal-based trading profits

sections.heading: The Honest Truth
sections.body:
Let's be real: "potential earnings" on our signal cards are **optimistic scenarios**. They're what could happen IF everything plays out perfectly. In reality, you'll rarely capture the full upside.

A good rule: **take profits at 50% of the indicated potential.** Greed is how beginners lose money.

Here's the realistic breakdown by signal type:

sections.heading: SEC Comment Signals
sections.body:
**Potential: 5-40% upside**
**Timeframe: 2-8 weeks**
**Difficulty: Easy to Medium**
**Capital: $100-$10K**

Realistic scenario:
- High materiality comment (score 8+): Stock drops 10-30% over 2-4 weeks
- If you short: capture 10-20% profit (not the full drop — you won't get perfect timing)
- If you stay away: you avoid the drop, which is itself a win

**Win rate:** About 55-60% of high-materiality comments lead to meaningful stock declines within 8 weeks

**Key insight:** You don't need to predict every outcome. You need an edge that compounds over many trades.

sections.heading: Patent Cliff Signals
sections.body:
**Potential: 20-80% (short the brand name)**
**Timeframe: 6-18 months**
**Difficulty: Medium to Hard**
**Capital: $1K-$10K+**

Realistic scenario:
- Brand-name stock drops 20-40% as cliff approaches
- Generic company stock rises 10-30% as it prepares to launch
- The biggest moves happen 6-12 months BEFORE the actual cliff

**Win rate:** About 65-70% of major patent cliffs result in significant brand-name revenue declines

**Key insight:** This is a longer-term play. Patience is required. The market is often slow to price in known cliffs.

sections.heading: Insider Trading Signals
sections.body:
**Potential: 5-30% over 6 months**
**Timeframe: 1-6 months**
**Difficulty: Easy**
**Capital: $100-$1K**

Realistic scenario:
- Cluster buys (3+ insiders): Stock rises 5-15% over 3-6 months
- Single large insider buy: Stock rises 3-8% over 3-6 months
- The edge is small but consistent

**Win rate:** About 55-60% for cluster buys, 52-55% for individual buys

**Key insight:** Insider trading signals work best as part of a diversified strategy, not as a standalone strategy.

sections.heading: The Math of Compounding
sections.body:
Here's how the math works:

If you have a 55% win rate and you make 10% on winners, lose 5% on losers:
- 100 trades: 55 wins × 10% = +550%
- 100 trades: 45 losses × 5% = -225%
- Net: +325% over 100 trades
- Per trade average: +3.25%

That's a **massive** edge. Most professional fund managers would kill for +3% per trade.

**The secret isn't picking winners. It's managing risk so your winners outpace your losers.**

This is why we show you:
- Difficulty level (so you know what you're getting into)
- Minimum capital (so you don't overextend)
- Chance of fail (so you size your positions correctly)

trading-guide.title: How to Actually Trade These Signals
trading-guide.subtitle: A step-by-step playbook for turning intelligence into profit

sections.heading: Step 1: Understand What You're Trading
sections.body:
You're not trading "tips." You're trading **information asymmetry**.

The information is public. But most people don't read it. That's your edge.

When you see a signal on our dashboard, you're seeing something that:
- Is legally public (on EDGAR)
- Required significant effort to produce (the SEC reviewed the filing)
- Is scored for materiality (our system filtered out the noise)
- Is actionable (you can actually do something with it)

This is not insider information. This is **superior processing of public information**.

sections.heading: Step 2: Assess the Signal
sections.body:
For each signal, check:

**Materiality Score (1-10):**
- 8-10: Serious SEC concern. High probability of market impact.
- 5-7: Moderate concern. May or may not move the stock.
- 1-4: Routine SEC review. Probably nothing.

**Difficulty Level:**
- Easy: Buy/sell stock on Robinhood. Anyone can do this.
- Medium: Options trading. Requires approval + knowledge.
- Hard: Shorting. Requires margin account + understanding of borrow costs.

**Capital Needed:**
- Don't overextend. A good rule: never risk more than 5% of your total portfolio on one signal.

**Timeframe:**
- Don't expect overnight moves. Give signals time to play out.
- Set a reminder to check back in the indicated timeframe.

sections.heading: Step 3: The Trade
sections.body:
**For SEC Comment Signals (Bearish):**
1. Read the comment letter (linked on the card)
2. Assess: Is this serious or routine?
3. If serious → Consider shorting or buying puts
4. If routine → The stock might actually be a buy (overreaction)
5. Set a stop loss at 5-10% above entry
6. Take profits at 50% of indicated potential

**For Patent Cliff Signals (Bearish on brand, bullish on generics):**
1. Identify the cliff date
2. Check if it's already priced in (has the stock already dropped?)
3. If not priced in → Short the brand name or buy puts
4. Alternatively → Long the generic company expected to launch
5. Hold for 6-18 months

**For Insider Trading Signals (Bullish):**
1. Verify it's a cluster buy (3+ insiders)
2. Check the purchase size (larger = stronger signal)
3. Buy the stock
4. Hold for 3-6 months
5. Set a stop loss at 5-10% below entry

sections.heading: Step 4: Risk Management
sections.body:
This is the most important step. Seriously.

**The Golden Rules:**
1. **Never invest more than you can afford to lose**
2. **Always use stop losses** — 5-10% on longs, 5-10% on shorts
3. **Diversify** — Don't bet everything on one signal
4. **Take profits early** — 50% of potential is the target
5. **Don't fall in love with positions** — If the thesis changes, exit

**Position Sizing:**
- High materiality (8-10): Max 10% of portfolio
- Medium materiality (5-7): Max 5% of portfolio
- Low materiality (1-4): Max 2% of portfolio

**The Math:**
If you risk 5% per trade and have a 55% win rate:
- 10 losses in a row: -50% (devastating but recoverable)
- 20 trades with 55% win rate: +10% net
- 50 trades with 55% win rate: +25% net
- 100 trades with 55% win rate: +50% net

**Patience + Math = Profit**
